lessons.title: The Concept of State
lessons.content:
A program without memory is a calculator. It processes input, returns output, and immediately forgets everything.

To build systems that track users, scores, or time, a program needs 'State'. State is data stored in your computer's short-term memory (RAM) while the program runs.

In JavaScript, we allocate this memory by declaring variables. You are reserving a microscopic physical space on a computer chip and giving it a label.

questions.q: Which keyword should you use to declare a value that will never change?
If you need a counter that goes up by one every time a user clicks, how should you declare it?
What happens if you try to redefine a `const` variable later in your code?
options: The program crashes and throws a TypeError.
It overwrites the old memory slot.

questions.q: Which of these represents a 'String' in JavaScript?
What is the specific data type of `false`?
If you declare `let score;` but do not assign a value, what does JavaScript store in that memory slot by default?

lessons.title: Conditional Execution
lessons.content:
Code executes strictly from top to bottom. However, a rigid script is useless in the real world. A system must adapt to user input.

We introduce flexibility using conditional statements (`if`, `else`). This creates forks in the road. The engine evaluates a condition—if it resolves to `true`, it takes one path. If `false`, it takes another.

This is how you build security gates, permissions, and responsive user interfaces.

questions.q: Which operator checks if two values are identical in both value AND data type?
What is the exact difference between `=` and `==`?
options: `=` is for text, `==` is for numbers.
`=` assigns a value to memory, `==` compares two values.

questions.q: Which logical operator requires BOTH conditions to be true? (e.g., The user is an admin AND the system is unlocked).
Which loop is guaranteed to execute its code block at least once, even if the condition is false?
In a standard loop (`let i=0; i<5; i++`), what does `i++` do?
Which keyword immediately aborts a loop entirely, skipping any remaining cycles?

lessons.title: Reusable Architecture
lessons.content:
Copying and pasting the same block of code multiple times is an engineering failure. If you need to change how that code works later, you have to hunt down every copy and fix it manually.

Functions solve this. A function is a self-contained block of logic. You write it once, and you 'call' it whenever you need it.

Think of a function like a machine: you feed it raw materials (parameters), it does the work inside its chassis, and it hands you the finished product (the return value).

questions.q: If you have a function named `authenticateUser`, how do you trigger it to run?
Which keyword must be used to extract the final result out of a function so the rest of your program can use it?
If a function executes successfully but lacks a `return` statement, what value does it hand back to the system?

lessons.title: Scope and Memory Borders
lessons.content:
Scope dictates where memory can be accessed.

If you declare a variable inside a function, it is 'locally scoped'. It exists only inside that function. The moment the function finishes running, the JavaScript engine destroys that variable to free up RAM.

Variables declared outside of any function are 'globally scoped' and can be seen by the entire system. Master engineers keep the global scope as clean as possible to prevent data collisions.

lessons.title: Organizing Complexity
lessons.content:
Variables are fine for single values. But what if you need to store a list of 10,000 enrolled students? You cannot write 10,000 variables.

We solve this with Data Structures. In JavaScript, the two primary structures are Arrays and Objects.

An Array is a strictly ordered list. Use it when sequence matters. An Object is a collection of labeled properties (Key-Value pairs). Use it when you need to describe a complex entity, like a user's profile.

questions.q: How do you access the very first item in an Array named `records`?
Which array method pushes a new item onto the absolute end of the list?
If you have an Object named `user`, how do you read its `email` property?

lessons.title: Bridging Logic and Pixels
lessons.content:
JavaScript running in a vacuum is invisible. To make an app interactive, JavaScript must talk to the HTML.

When a browser loads a webpage, it converts the HTML into a tree of data called the Document Object Model (DOM). JavaScript can select branches of this tree, read their data, change their colors, or destroy them entirely.

By attaching 'Event Listeners' to these branches, JavaScript can wait for a user to click, type, or scroll, and instantly react.

questions.q: Which method is the modern standard for finding an HTML element using its CSS class or ID?
How do you listen for a user clicking on a button stored in a variable named `submitBtn`?
Which property allows you to completely replace the text and HTML inside a selected `div`?

lessons.title: The Single Thread
lessons.content:
JavaScript is 'single-threaded'. It has only one worker. It can only do one task at a time.

If your code asks a server for a heavy database file, and that takes 5 seconds, the entire website will freeze. The user won't even be able to click a button.

We solve this with 'Asynchronous' code. When JavaScript hits a slow task, it hands the task off to the browser in the background, and continues executing the rest of your code. When the background task finishes, it taps JavaScript on the shoulder with a 'Promise'.

questions.q: What object represents the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation?
To pause execution inside a function until a Promise resolves, which keyword do you use?
If you want to use the `await` keyword, what MUST you put in front of the function declaration?

lessons.title: The Fetch API
lessons.content:
Modern applications do not hardcode their data. They retrieve it dynamically from databases across the world using APIs (Application Programming Interfaces).

In JavaScript, we make these network requests using the `fetch()` function. 

Because network speed is entirely unpredictable, `fetch()` is inherently asynchronous. It returns a Promise. You must `await` the server's response, and then you must parse that raw text response into usable JavaScript Objects, usually via JSON.

questions.q: Which function natively makes HTTP requests to external servers in modern JavaScript?
After fetching data from a modern API, which method is typically used to parse the raw response into a JavaScript object?
What does JSON stand for?

lessons.title: Declarative Architecture
lessons.content:
Standard `for` loops are manual labor. They require you to set up counters, manage length logic, and manually push data into new arrays.

Master engineers use 'Higher Order Array Methods' like `.map()`, `.filter()`, and `.reduce()`. These are declarative. You do not tell the computer *how* to iterate; you simply tell it *what* you want the final data to look like.

This leads to code that is vastly easier to read, test, and debug.

questions.q: Which array method takes an original array, transforms every item in it, and returns a brand new array of the exact same length?
If you have an array of 100 users, and you only want an array of users who are older than 18, which method should you use?
Which method iterates through an array and collapses all of its values down into a single final output (like a sum or total)?

lessons.title: Object Oriented Classes
lessons.content:
As applications scale, manually creating hundreds of loose objects becomes chaotic. You need standardized templates.

A `class` is a blueprint. You write the blueprint once, and then use the `new` keyword to stamp out as many identical Objects as you need. Each object created from a class is called an 'instance'.

Inside a class, variables are called 'properties' and functions are called 'methods'. To reference an object's own internal data, you must use the `this` keyword.

questions.q: Which special method inside a `class` is automatically triggered the exact moment a new instance is created?
To create a new user from a `User` class blueprint, what syntax do you use?
Inside a class method, which keyword refers to the specific instance of the object that is currently executing the code?

lessons.title: Graceful Failure
lessons.content:
All systems fail. Networks drop, servers crash, and users input invalid data. An amateur's script will crash the entire page and display a white screen.

A master engineer anticipates failure and handles it gracefully. We do this using `try / catch` blocks. 

You place risky code (like network requests) inside the `try` block. If the code detonates, the system does not crash. Instead, it immediately hands the error to the `catch` block, allowing you to log the issue and show the user a friendly warning.

questions.q: What block of code will execute if the code inside the `try` block fails?
Which block of code executes at the very end, regardless of whether the `try` succeeded or failed?
How can you manually trigger your own custom error to stop execution?
